    27→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    28→
    29→/-- Oriented angles make sense in 2d.
    30→
    31→Note: this can't blindly be added to mathlib as it creates an "instance diamond"
    32→with an instance for modules satisfying `is_empty`. -/
    33→noncomputable instance Module.orientedEuclideanSpaceFinTwo : Module.Oriented ℝ ℝ² (Fin 2) :=
    34→  ⟨Basis.orientation <| PiLp.basisFun 2 _ _⟩
    35→
    36→/-- Two dimensional euclidean space is two-dimensional. -/
    37→instance fact_finrank_euclideanSpace_fin_two : Fact (Module.finrank ℝ ℝ² = 2) :=
    38→  ⟨finrank_euclideanSpace_fin⟩

    49→
    50→variable [Module.Oriented ℝ V (Fin 2)] [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ V = 2)] {p : Fin n → P}
    51→
    52→/-- We say a subset `A` of points in the plane is non-trilinear
    53→if it contains no three points that lie on the same line. -/
    54→def NonTrilinear (A : Set P) : Prop :=
    55→  A.Triplewise (fun x y z ↦ ¬ Collinear ℝ {x, y, z})
    56→
    57→/-- `ConvexIndep S` means that `S` consists of extremal points of its convex hull,
    58→i.e., the point set encloses a convex shape.
    59→Also known as a "convex-independent set". -/
    60→def ConvexIndep (S : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    61→  ∀ a ∈ S, a ∉ convexHull ℝ (S \ {a})
    62→
    63→/-- The set `P` contains a convex `n`-gon.
    64→See also `IsConvexPolygon`. -/
    65→def HasConvexNGon (n : ℕ) (P : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    66→  ∃ S : Finset ℝ², S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ P ∧ ConvexIndep S
    67→
    68→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a counter-clockwise convex polygon. -/
    69→def IsCcwConvexPolygon (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
    70→  ∀ ⦃i j k⦄, i < j → j < k → (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1
    71→
    72→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    73→  (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1 := hp hij hjk
    74→
    75→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    76→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    77→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p j) (p k) (p i)).sign = 1 := by
    78→  rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    79→  exact hp hij hjk
    80→
    81→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    82→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle'' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    83→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p k) (p i) (p j)).sign = 1 := by
    84→  rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    85→  exact hp hij hjk
    86→
    87→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle_finRotate (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p)
    88→    (hn : 3 ≤ n) (i : Fin n) :
    89→    (∡ (p i) (p <| finRotate _ i) (p <| finRotate _ (finRotate _ i))).sign = 1 := by
    90→  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := le_iff_exists_add'.mp hn
    91→  by_cases hi : i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    92→  · rw [hi, finRotate_last, finRotate_apply_zero]
    93→    exact hp.sign_oangle'' Fin.zero_lt_one Fin.one_lt_last
    94→  by_cases hi' : finRotate _ i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    95→  · rw [hi', finRotate_last]
    96→    refine hp.sign_oangle' ?_ ((Fin.le_last _).lt_of_ne hi)
    97→    rw [Fin.pos_iff_ne_zero]
    98→    rintro rfl
    99→    rw [finRotate_apply_zero] at hi'
   100→    exact Fin.one_lt_last.ne hi'
   101→  apply hp.sign_oangle <;> apply lt_finRotate_of_ne_last <;> assumption
   102→
   103→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_zero (p : Fin 0 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := finZeroElim
   104→
   105→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_one (p : Fin 1 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   106→
   107→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_two (p : Fin 2 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   108→
   109→set_option linter.docPrime false in
   110→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four' {p : Fin 4 → P} :
   111→    IsCcwConvexPolygon p ↔ (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 1) (p 2) (p 3)).sign = 1 ∧
   112→    (∡ (p 2) (p 3) (p 0)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 3) (p 0) (p 1)).sign = 1 := by
   113→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨h1, h2, h3, h4⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
   114→  · obtain ⟨h01, h12, h23⟩ : (0 : Fin 4) < 1 ∧ (1 : Fin 4) < 2 ∧ (2 : Fin 4) < 3 := by simp
   115→    · repeat' constructor
   116→      · exact h.sign_oangle h01 h12
   117→      · exact h.sign_oangle h12 h23
   118→      · exact h.sign_oangle' (h01.trans h12) h23
   119→      · exact h.sign_oangle'' h01 (h12.trans h23)
   120→  · intro i j k hij hjk
   121→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   122→    · exact h1
   123→    · rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   124→      exact h4
   125→    · rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   126→      exact h3
   127→    · exact h2
   128→
   129→@[simp]
   130→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   131→    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] ↔
   132→      (∡ A B C).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ B C D).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ C D A).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ D A B).sign = 1 :=
   133→  isCcwConvexPolygon_four'
   134→
   135→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/
   136→def IsConvexPolygon {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
   137→  IsCcwConvexPolygon p ∨ IsCcwConvexPolygon fun i => p (-i)
   138→
   139→/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   140→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   141→    (hABC : AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   142→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   143→  cases hsABC : (∡ A B C).sign
   144→  · exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   145→  · right
   146→    intro i j k hij hjk
   147→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   148→    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   149→    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   150→  · left
   151→    intro i j k hij hjk
   152→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   153→    exact hsABC
   154→
   155→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   156→    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] ↔ AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C] := by
   157→  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent⟩
   158→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   159→  let p := ![A, B, C]
   160→  change IsConvexPolygon p at h
   161→  change Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ≠ 0
   162→  cases h with
   163→  | inl h =>
   164→    rw [h.sign_oangle (by simp) (by simp)]
   165→    rintro ⟨⟩
   166→  | inr h =>
   167→    suffices Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 2) (p 1)) = 1 by rw [← oangle_swap₂₃_sign, this]; rintro ⟨⟩
   168→    exact h.sign_oangle (i := 0) (j := 1) (k := 2) (by simp) (by simp)
   169→
   170→theorem isConvexPolygon_triangle (t : Affine.Triangle ℝ P) : IsConvexPolygon t.points := by
   171→  have : t.points = ![t.points 0, t.points 1, t.points 2] := by ext i; fin_cases i <;> rfl
   172→  rw [this, isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent, ← this]
   173→  exact t.independent
   174→
   175→noncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ℝ :=
   176→  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ᵥ c) (b -ᵥ c) / 2
   177→
   178→lemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ℝ²) :
   179→    triangle_area a b c =
   180→    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   181→                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   182→                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   183→  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   184→    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ℝ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   185→  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   186→      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   187→    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   188→  ring
   189→
   190→/--
   191→Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   192→of points.
   193→-/
   194→noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   195→  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ℝ² × ℝ²) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   196→
   197→end EuclideanGeometry
   198→
   199→
   200→def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
   201→  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q
   202→
   203→nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
   204→  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
   205→

    16→import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Sym
    17→import Mathlib.Topology.MetricSpace.Defs
    18→
    19→open scoped Finset
    20→
    21→variable {X : Type*} [MetricSpace X]
    22→
    23→/-- The number of pairs of points of a finite set `s` in a metric space that are distance 1 apart.
    24→-/
    25→noncomputable def unitDistNum (s : Finset X) : ℕ := #{p ∈ s.sym2 | dist p.out.1 p.out.2 = 1}

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→
     4→Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     5→you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     6→You may obtain a copy of the License at
     7→

     9→

    11→distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 91
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/91](https://www.erdosproblems.com/91)
    23→
    24→For large $n$, is it true that every $n$-point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$ minimizing the number of
    25→distinct distances admits another minimizer of the same size that is not similar to it?
    26→
    27→[Er87b] Erdős, P., _Some combinatorial, probabilistic and number theoretic problems_ (1987), p.171.
    28→
    29→[Er90] Erdős, P., (1990).
    30→
    31→[Er97e] Erdős, P., (1997).
    32→
    33→[Ko24c] Kovács, (2024).
    34→-/
    35→
    36→open Finset Classical
    37→
    38→namespace Erdos91
    39→
    40→/--
    41→The number of distinct positive distances determined by a finite point set $A$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    42→-/
    43→noncomputable def numDistinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    44→  ((A ×ˢ A).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2)).image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2) |>.card
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Two finite point sets in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are similar if there exists a map
    48→$f : \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ that scales all distances by the same positive constant $r$
    49→and maps one set onto the other.
    50→-/
    51→def AreSimilar (A B : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    52→  ∃ (f : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2) → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (r : ℝ),
    53→    r > 0 ∧
    54→    (∀ x y : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2), dist (f x) (f y) = r * dist x y) ∧
    55→    (∀ a, a ∈ A → f a ∈ B) ∧
    56→    (∀ b, b ∈ B → ∃ a ∈ A, f a = b)
    57→
    58→/--
    59→Erdős Problem 91 [Er87b, Er90, Er97e]: For sufficiently large $n$, if
    60→$A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ has $|A| = n$ and minimises the number of distinct distances, then there
    61→exists another minimiser $A'$ of the same cardinality that is not similar to $A$. In other words,
    62→there are at least two non-similar sets that minimise the number of distinct distances.
    63→-/
    64→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    65→theorem erdos_91 : answer(sorry) ↔
    66→    ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    67→    ∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    68→      A.card = n →
    69→      (∀ B : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), B.card = n →
    70→        numDistinctDistances A ≤ numDistinctDistances B) →
    71→      ∃ A' : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    72→        A'.card = n ∧
    73→        (∀ B : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), B.card = n →
    74→          numDistinctDistances A' ≤ numDistinctDistances B) ∧
    75→        ¬ AreSimilar A A' := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos91
    79→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 93
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/93](https://www.erdosproblems.com/93)
    23→
    24→If $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are in convex position (vertices of a convex polygon), then they
    25→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman.
    26→
    27→[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_. Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148-157.
    28→-/
    29→
    30→open scoped Classical
    31→
    32→namespace Erdos93
    33→
    34→/--
    35→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is in convex position if no point lies in the
    36→convex hull of the remaining points. Equivalently, the points are the vertices
    37→of a convex polygon.
    38→-/
    39→def InConvexPosition (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    40→  ∀ p ∈ A, p ∉ convexHull ℝ ((↑A : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) \ {p})
    41→
    42→/--
    43→The set of distinct pairwise distances between distinct points in a finite
    44→point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    47→  A.offDiag.image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2)
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 93: If $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a convex polygon, then they
    51→determine at least $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ distinct distances. Proved by Altman [Al63].
    52→-/
    53→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    54→theorem erdos_93
    55→    (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)))
    56→    (hconv : InConvexPosition A)
    57→    (hn : 2 ≤ A.card) :
    58→    A.card / 2 ≤ (distinctDistances A).card := by
    59→  sorry
    60→
    61→end Erdos93
    62→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 95
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/95](https://www.erdosproblems.com/95)
    23→
    24→For $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ with distance multiplicities $f(u_i)$, the sum
    25→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll_\varepsilon n^{3+\varepsilon}$ for every $\varepsilon > 0$.
    26→Proved by Guth and Katz (2015) with the stronger bound $O(n^3 \log n)$.
    27→
    28→[GuKa15] Guth, L. and Katz, N.H., _On the Erdős distinct distances problem in the plane_,
    29→Annals of Mathematics **181** (2015), 155–190.
    30→-/
    31→
    32→open Classical
    33→
    34→namespace Erdos95
    35→
    36→/--
    37→The set of distinct distances determined by a finite point set $P$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    38→-/
    39→noncomputable def distinctDistances (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    40→  ((P.product P).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2)).image (fun pq => dist pq.1 pq.2)
    41→
    42→/--
    43→The number of ordered pairs $(p, q)$ from $P$ with $p \neq q$ and $\operatorname{dist}(p, q) = d$.
    44→-/
    45→noncomputable def distMultiplicity (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) (d : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    46→  ((P.product P).filter (fun pq => pq.1 ≠ pq.2 ∧ dist pq.1 pq.2 = d)).card
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ determine the set of distances $\{u_1, \ldots, u_t\}$.
    50→Suppose $u_i$ appears as the distance between $f(u_i)$ many pairs of points. Then for all
    51→$\varepsilon > 0$,
    52→$$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll_\varepsilon n^{3+\varepsilon}.$$
    53→
    54→This was proved by Guth and Katz (2015) who showed the stronger bound
    55→$\sum_i f(u_i)^2 \ll n^3 \log n$.
    56→-/
    57→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    58→theorem erdos_95 :
    59→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    60→      ∃ C : ℝ, C > 0 ∧
    61→        ∀ (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))),
    62→          ((distinctDistances P).sum
    63→            (fun d => (distMultiplicity P d) ^ 2) : ℝ) ≤
    64→            C * (P.card : ℝ) ^ ((3 : ℝ) + ε) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→end Erdos95
    68→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 89
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/89](https://www.erdosproblems.com/89)
    23→-/
    24→
    25→open Filter
    26→open EuclideanGeometry
    27→
    28→namespace Erdos89
    29→
    30→/--
    31→The minimum number of distinct distances guaranteed for any set of $n$ points.
    32→-/
    33→noncomputable def minimalDistinctDistances (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    34→  sInf {(distinctDistances points : ℝ) | (points : Finset ℝ²) (_ : points.card = n)}
    35→
    36→/--
    37→Does every set of $n$ distinct points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ determine $\gg \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$
    38→many distinct distances?
    39→-/
    40→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    41→theorem erdos_89 :
    42→    (fun (n : ℕ) => n/(n : ℝ).log.sqrt) =O[atTop] (fun n => (minimalDistinctDistances n : ℝ)) := by
    43→  sorry
    44→
    45→/--
    46→Guth and Katz [GuKa15] proved that there are always $\gg \frac{n}{\log n}$ many distinct distances.
    47→
    48→[GuKa15] Guth, Larry and Katz, Nets Hawk, On the Erdős distinct distances problem in the plane. Ann. of Math. (2) (2015), 155-190.
    49→-/
    50→@[category research solved, AMS 52]
    51→theorem erdos_89.variants.n_dvd_log_n :
    52→    (fun (n : ℕ) => n/(n : ℝ).log) =O[atTop] (fun n => (minimalDistinctDistances n : ℝ)) := by
    53→  sorry
    54→
    55→/--
    56→This theorem provides a sanity check, showing that the main conjecture (`erdos_89`) is strictly
    57→stronger than the solved Guth and Katz result. It proves that, trivially, if the lower bound
    58→$\frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$ holds, then the weaker lower bound $\frac{n}{\log n}$ must also hold.
    59→-/
    60→@[category test, AMS 52]
    61→theorem erdos_89.variants.implies_n_dvd_log_n (h : type_of% erdos_89) :
    62→    type_of% erdos_89.variants.n_dvd_log_n := by
    63→  refine .trans ?_ h
    64→  have := (Asymptotics.isLittleO_one_left_iff ℝ).mpr <| tendsto_norm_atTop_atTop.comp <|
    65→    (tendsto_rpow_atTop (show 0 < 1/2 by norm_num)).comp
    66→    (Real.tendsto_log_atTop.comp tendsto_natCast_atTop_atTop)
    67→  convert (Asymptotics.isBigO_refl (fun n : ℕ ↦ n/(n : ℝ).log) _).mul this.isBigO using 1
    68→  · simp
    69→  · simp_rw [Function.comp, div_mul, ← Real.sqrt_eq_rpow, Real.div_sqrt]
    70→
    71→
    72→-- TODO(firsching): formalize the rest of the remarks
    73→
    74→end Erdos89
    75→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 653
    21→
    22→For $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$, let $R(x_i)$ count the number of distinct distances from
    23→$x_i$ to all other points. Let $g(n)$ be the maximum number of distinct values the $R(x_i)$
    24→can take. Is it true that $g(n) \geq (1 - o(1))n$?
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/653](https://www.erdosproblems.com/653)
    27→
    28→[Er97e] Erdős, P., *Some of my favourite problems which recently have been solved*.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→open Finset Classical
    32→
    33→namespace Erdos653
    34→
    35→/--
    36→For a configuration of $n$ points in the Euclidean plane, $R(x_i)$ counts the number
    37→of distinct distances from $x_i$ to all other points.
    38→-/
    39→noncomputable def distinctDistances (n : ℕ) (x : Fin n → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))
    40→    (i : Fin n) : ℕ :=
    41→  ((Finset.univ.filter (· ≠ i)).image (fun j => dist (x i) (x j))).card
    42→
    43→/--
    44→For a configuration of $n$ points in the Euclidean plane, the number of distinct
    45→values that $R(x_i)$ takes over all $i$.
    46→-/
    47→noncomputable def numDistinctR (n : ℕ) (x : Fin n → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) : ℕ :=
    48→  (Finset.univ.image (fun i => distinctDistances n x i)).card
    49→
    50→/--
    51→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and
    52→$R(x_i) = \#\{|x_j - x_i| : j \neq i\}$. Order the points so that
    53→$R(x_1) \leq \cdots \leq R(x_n)$. Let $g(n)$ be the maximum number of distinct
    54→values the $R(x_i)$ can take. Is it true that $g(n) \geq (1 - o(1))n$? [Er97e]
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    57→theorem erdos_653 :
    58→    answer(sorry) ↔
    59→      ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    60→        ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, N ≤ n →
    61→          ∃ x : Fin n → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2),
    62→            (numDistinctR n x : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * n := by
    63→  sorry
    64→
    65→end Erdos653
    66→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 652
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/652](https://www.erdosproblems.com/652)
    23→
    24→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ and let
    25→$R(x_i) = \#\{ |x_j - x_i| : j \neq i \}$, where the points are ordered such that
    26→$R(x_1) \leq \cdots \leq R(x_n)$.
    27→
    28→Let $\alpha_k$ be minimal such that, for all large enough $n$, there exists a set of
    29→$n$ points with $R(x_k) < \alpha_k n^{1/2}$.
    30→
    31→Is it true that $\alpha_k \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$?
    32→
    33→This was proved in the affirmative. Mathialagan [Ma21] showed that given sets $P$
    34→($k$ points) and $Q$ ($n$ points) with $2 \leq k \leq n^{1/3}$, some point of $P$
    35→determines $\gg (kn)^{1/2}$ distances to $Q$, implying
    36→$R(x_k) \gg (kn)^{1/2}$ for $2 \leq k \leq n^{1/3}$.
    37→
    38→[Ma21] Mathialagan, S., *Distinct distances for points along a curve in the plane*. 2021.
    39→-/
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos652
    42→
    43→/--
    44→The number of distinct distances from a point $p$ to other points in a finite set
    45→$S \subset \mathbb{R}^2$. That is, $R(p) = \#\{\operatorname{dist}(p, q) : q \in S, q \neq p\}$.
    46→-/
    47→noncomputable def numDistinctDistances (p : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))
    48→    (S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    49→  ((S.filter (· ≠ p)).image (dist p)).card
    50→
    51→/--
    52→Erdős Problem 652: The conjecture that $\alpha_k \to \infty$ is equivalent to: for every
    53→constant $C > 0$, for all sufficiently large $k$, there are infinitely many $n$ such that
    54→every set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ has fewer than $k$ points with fewer than
    55→$C\sqrt{n}$ distinct distances to the rest of the set.
    56→
    57→This was proved in the affirmative by Mathialagan [Ma21].
    58→-/
    59→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    60→theorem erdos_652 :
    61→    answer(True) ↔
    62→      ∀ C : ℝ, C > 0 →
    63→        ∃ K : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, k ≥ K →
    64→          ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n : ℕ, n ≥ N ∧
    65→            ∀ S : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), S.card = n →
    66→              (S.filter (fun p =>
    67→                (numDistinctDistances p S : ℝ) < C * Real.sqrt (↑n))).card < k := by
    68→  sorry
    69→
    70→end Erdos652
    71→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 655
    21→
    22→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ be such that no circle centered at one of the $x_i$
    23→passes through three others. Are there at least $(1 + c) n / 2$ distinct distances for some
    24→constant $c > 0$ and all $n$ sufficiently large?
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/655](https://www.erdosproblems.com/655)
    27→-/
    28→
    29→namespace Erdos655
    30→
    31→/--
    32→A finite set of points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the "no three equidistant from a center"
    33→condition if for each point $p$ in the set, no three other distinct points in the
    34→set are equidistant from $p$ (i.e., no circle centered at $p$ passes through three
    35→or more other points).
    36→-/
    37→def NoThreeEquidistantFromCenter (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    38→  ∀ p ∈ P, ∀ q₁ ∈ P, ∀ q₂ ∈ P, ∀ q₃ ∈ P,
    39→    p ≠ q₁ → p ≠ q₂ → p ≠ q₃ →
    40→    q₁ ≠ q₂ → q₁ ≠ q₃ → q₂ ≠ q₃ →
    41→    ¬(dist p q₁ = dist p q₂ ∧ dist p q₂ = dist p q₃)
    42→
    43→/--
    44→The number of distinct pairwise distances determined by a finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$.
    45→-/
    46→noncomputable def distinctDistanceCount (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    47→  Set.ncard {d : ℝ | ∃ p ∈ P, ∃ q ∈ P, p ≠ q ∧ d = dist p q}
    48→
    49→/--
    50→Erdős Problem 655 (Erdős–Pach):
    51→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ be such that no circle whose centre is one of the $x_i$
    52→contains three other points. Then the number of distinct distances determined
    53→by the points is at least $(1 + c) \cdot n / 2$ for some constant $c > 0$ and all $n$
    54→sufficiently large.
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    57→theorem erdos_655 : answer(sorry) ↔
    58→    ∃ c : ℝ, c > 0 ∧
    59→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    60→        ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    61→          P.card = n →
    62→          NoThreeEquidistantFromCenter P →
    63→          (distinctDistanceCount P : ℝ) ≥ (1 + c) * (n : ℝ) / 2 := by
    64→  sorry
    65→
    66→end Erdos655
    67→

1→/-
     2→Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.
     3→

    12→WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 958
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/958](https://www.erdosproblems.com/958)
    23→
    24→Let $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ be a finite set of size $n$, and let $\{d_1, \ldots, d_k\}$
    25→be the set of distinct distances determined by $A$. Let $f(d)$ be the multiplicity of $d$,
    26→the number of unordered pairs from $A$ at distance $d$ apart.
    27→
    28→Is it true that $k = n - 1$ and $\{f(d_i)\} = \{1, \ldots, n-1\}$ if and only if $A$ is
    29→a set of equidistant points on a line or a circle?
    30→
    31→Erdős conjectured that the answer is no, and other such configurations exist [Er84c].
    32→
    33→This was proved by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25], who observed that equidistant
    34→points on a short circular arc on a circle of radius 1, together with the centre, provide
    35→a counterexample.
    36→
    37→[Er84c] Erdős, P., _Some old and new problems on combinatorial geometry_, 1984.
    38→
    39→[CDL25] Clemen, F., Dumitrescu, A., and Liu, Y., 2025.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos958
    43→
    44→/-- The set of distinct distances determined by a finite point set in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    45→noncomputable def distinctDistances (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Finset ℝ :=
    46→  ((A ×ˢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ≠ p.2)).image (fun p => dist p.1 p.2)
    47→
    48→/-- The number of unordered pairs in $A$ at distance $d$. -/
    49→noncomputable def distMultiplicity (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) (d : ℝ) : ℕ :=
    50→  ((A ×ˢ A).filter (fun p => p.1 ≠ p.2 ∧ dist p.1 p.2 = d)).card / 2
    51→
    52→/-- The distance multiplicity property: $A$ has exactly $n-1$ distinct distances and the
    53→multiplicities are exactly $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$. -/
    54→def HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    55→  A.card ≥ 2 ∧
    56→  (distinctDistances A).card = A.card - 1 ∧
    57→  ∀ m : ℕ, (1 ≤ m ∧ m ≤ A.card - 1) ↔
    58→    (∃ d ∈ distinctDistances A, distMultiplicity A d = m)
    59→
    60→/-- A finite point set is equidistant-collinear if the points are equally spaced along a
    61→line: $A = \{p, p + v, p + 2v, \ldots, p + (n-1)v\}$ for some $p, v$ with $v \neq 0$. -/
    62→def IsEquidistantCollinear (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    63→  ∃ (p v : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)), v ≠ 0 ∧
    64→    ∀ a ∈ A, ∃ i : Fin A.card, a = p + (i.val : ℝ) • v
    65→
    66→/-- A finite point set is equidistant-circular if the points are equally spaced on a
    67→circle: all points lie on a circle and consecutive points in a cyclic ordering have
    68→equal Euclidean distance. -/
    69→def IsEquidistantCircular (A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : Prop :=
    70→  A.card ≥ 3 ∧
    71→  ∃ (c : EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)) (r : ℝ), r > 0 ∧
    72→    (∀ a ∈ A, dist a c = r) ∧
    73→    ∃ (σ : ℕ → EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    74→      (∀ i, i < A.card → σ i ∈ A) ∧
    75→      (∀ a ∈ A, ∃ i, i < A.card ∧ σ i = a) ∧
    76→      (∀ i j, i < A.card → j < A.card → σ i = σ j → i = j) ∧
    77→      ∀ i, i < A.card →
    78→        dist (σ i) (σ ((i + 1) % A.card)) = dist (σ 0) (σ 1)
    79→
    80→/--
    81→**Erdős Problem 958** [Er84c]:
    82→
    83→Is it true that if a finite set $A \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ satisfies the distance multiplicity
    84→property (exactly $n-1$ distinct distances with multiplicities $\{1, \ldots, n-1\}$), then $A$
    85→must be a set of equidistant points on a line or equidistant points on a circle?
    86→
    87→The answer is no. This was proved by Clemen, Dumitrescu, and Liu [CDL25].
    88→-/
    89→@[category research solved, AMS 5 52]
    90→theorem erdos_958 : answer(False) ↔
    91→    (∀ A : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    92→      HasDistanceMultiplicityProperty A →
    93→      (IsEquidistantCollinear A ∨ IsEquidistantCircular A)) := by
    94→  sorry
    95→
    96→end Erdos958
    97→

    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 661
    21→
    22→Erdős and Pach asked whether there exist, for all large $n$, point sets
    23→$x_1, \ldots, x_n, y_1, \ldots, y_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$ such that the number of distinct
    24→bipartite distances $d(x_i, y_j)$ is $o(n / \sqrt{\log n})$.
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/661](https://www.erdosproblems.com/661)
    27→
    28→[ErPa90] Erdős, P. and Pach, J. (1990)
    29→
    30→[Er92e] Erdős, P. (1992)
    31→
    32→[Er97e] Erdős, P. (1997)
    33→
    34→[Er97f] Erdős, P. (1997)
    35→-/
    36→
    37→open Real
    38→
    39→namespace Erdos661
    40→
    41→/-- The number of distinct bipartite distances between two finite point sets
    42→in $\mathbb{R}^2$, i.e., the number of distinct values $\operatorname{dist}(x, y)$
    43→for $x \in X$, $y \in Y$. -/
    44→noncomputable def bipartiteDistinctDistances
    45→    (X Y : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : ℕ :=
    46→  Set.ncard {d : ℝ | ∃ x ∈ X, ∃ y ∈ Y, d = dist x y}
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**Erdős Problem 661** [ErPa90, Er92e, Er97e, Er97f]:
    50→
    51→Are there, for all large $n$, some points $x_1, \ldots, x_n, y_1, \ldots, y_n \in \mathbb{R}^2$
    52→such that the number of distinct distances $d(x_i, y_j)$ is $o(n / \sqrt{\log n})$?
    53→
    54→Formulated as: for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$,
    55→there exist $X, Y \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ with $|X| = n$, $|Y| = n$, and the number of distinct
    56→bipartite distances is at most $\varepsilon \cdot n / \sqrt{\log n}$.
    57→-/
    58→@[category research open, AMS 5 52]
    59→theorem erdos_661 : answer(sorry) ↔
    60→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    61→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    62→        ∃ X Y : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)),
    63→          X.card = n ∧ Y.card = n ∧
    64→          (bipartiteDistinctDistances X Y : ℝ) ≤
    65→            ε * (n : ℝ) / Real.sqrt (Real.log (n : ℝ)) := by
    66→  sorry
    67→
    68→end Erdos661
    69→

    23→
    24→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^3$ be the vertices of a convex polyhedron. Are there at
    25→least $(1 - o(1))n/2$ many distinct distances between the $x_i$?
    26→
    27→For the similar problem in $\mathbb{R}^2$ there are always at least $n/2$ distances,
    28→as proved by Altman [Al63] (see [93]).

    33→[Al63] Altman, E., _On a problem of P. Erdős_, Amer. Math. Monthly 70 (1963), 148–157.

    35→
    36→namespace Erdos660
    37→
    38→/-- Points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ are in convex position if every point is a vertex of the
    39→    convex hull, i.e., no point lies in the convex hull of the remaining points. -/
    40→def InConvexPosition3d (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : Prop :=
    41→  ∀ p ∈ P, p ∉ convexHull ℝ (↑(P.erase p) : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)))
    42→
    43→/-- The number of distinct pairwise distances determined by a finite point set in
    44→    $\mathbb{R}^3$. -/
    45→noncomputable def distinctDistanceCount3d (P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3))) : ℕ :=
    46→  Set.ncard {d : ℝ | ∃ p ∈ P, ∃ q ∈ P, p ≠ q ∧ d = dist p q}
    47→
    48→/--
    49→**Erdős Problem 660** [Er97e, p.531]:
    50→
    51→Let $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \mathbb{R}^3$ be the vertices of a convex polyhedron. Then the number
    52→of distinct distances determined by these points is at least $(1 - o(1)) \cdot n/2$,
    53→i.e., for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$, the number of
    54→distinct distances is at least $(1 - \varepsilon) \cdot n/2$.
    55→-/
    56→@[category research open, AMS 52]
    57→theorem erdos_660 :
    58→    answer(sorry) ↔
    59→    ∀ ε : ℝ, ε > 0 →
    60→      ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n : ℕ, n ≥ N →
    61→        ∀ P : Finset (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 3)),
    62→          P.card = n →
    63→          InConvexPosition3d P →
    64→          (distinctDistanceCount3d P : ℝ) ≥ (1 - ε) * (n : ℝ) / 2 := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→end Erdos660

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 659
    21→
    22→*References:*
    23→- [erdosproblems.com/659](https://www.erdosproblems.com/659)
    24→- [MoOs06] Moree, Pieter and Osburn, Robert, Two-dimensional lattices with few distances. Enseign. Math. (2) (2006), 361--380
    25→- [ErFi96] Erdős, Paul and Fishburn, Peter, Maximum planar sets that determine {$k$} distances. Discrete Math. (1996), 115--125.
    26→- [Gr26](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.09102): Benjamin Grayzel, Solution to a Problem of Erdős Concerning Distances and Points
    27→-/
    28→
    29→open EuclideanGeometry Finset Real
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos659
    32→
    33→/--
    34→Is there a set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ such that every subset of $4$ points determines at
    35→least $3$ distances, yet the total number of distinct distances is $\ll \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$?
    36→
    37→There does exist such a set: a suitable truncation of the lattice
    38→$\{(a,b\sqrt{2}): a,b\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ suffices. This construction appears to have been first
    39→considered by Moree and Osburn \cite{MoOs06}, who proved that it has
    40→ $\ll \frac{n}{\sqrt{\log n}}$ many distinct distances. This construction was independently found by
    41→ [Lund and Sheffer](https://adamsheffer.wordpress.com/2014/07/16/point-sets-with-few-distinct-distances/),
    42→ who further noted that this configuration contains no squares or equilateral triangles.
    43→
    44→There are only six possible configurations of $4$ points which determine only $2$ distances
    45→(first noted by Erdős and Fishburn [ErFi96]), and five of them contain either a square or an
    46→equilateral triangle. The remaining configuration contains four points from a regular pentagon,
    47→and Grayzel [Gr26] (using Gemini) has noted in the comments that this configuration can also be
    48→ruled out, thus giving a complete solution to this problem. Boris Alexeev using Aristotle provides
    49→a formalisation of the proof.
    50→-/
    51→@[category research formally solved using lean4 at "https://github.com/plby/lean-proofs/blob/226d5fad7143dcebea2bbb5ec87f18a3a1dcea69/src/v4.24.0/ErdosProblems/Erdos659.lean", AMS 52]
    52→theorem erdos_659 : answer(True) ↔ ∃ A : ℕ → Finset ℝ²,
    53→   (∀ n, #(A n) = n ∧ ∀ S ⊆ A n, #S = 4 → 3 ≤ distinctDistances S) ∧
    54→    (fun n ↦ distinctDistances (A n)) ≪ fun n ↦ n / sqrt (log n) := by
    55→  sorry
    56→
    57→end Erdos659
    58→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1017
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1017](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1017)
    23→
    24→Let $f(n,k)$ be such that every graph on $n$ vertices and $k$ edges can be
    25→partitioned into at most $f(n,k)$ edge-disjoint complete graphs. Estimate
    26→$f(n,k)$ for $k > n^2/4$.
    27→
    28→The function $f(n,k)$ is sometimes called the clique partition number.
    29→
    30→Erdős, Goodman, and Pósa [EGP66] proved that $f(n,k) \le \lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ for all $k$
    31→(and in fact edges and triangles suffice), which is best possible in general,
    32→as witnessed by a complete bipartite graph. Erdős [Er71] asks whether this
    33→bound can be sharpened when $k > n^2/4$.
    34→
    35→[EGP66] Erdős, P., Goodman, A. W., and Pósa, L., _The representation of a graph by set
    36→intersections_. Canadian Journal of Mathematics 18 (1966), 106-112.
    37→
    38→[Er71] Erdős, P., _Some unsolved problems in graph theory and combinatorial analysis_.
    39→Combinatorial Mathematics and its Applications (Proc. Conf., Oxford, 1969) (1971), 97-109.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Finset Filter
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos1017
    45→
    46→/--
    47→Erdős Problem 1017 [Er71]:
    48→
    49→Erdős asks whether the clique partition number $f(n,k)$ can be improved
    50→below $\lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ when the number of edges $k$ exceeds $n^2/4$.
    51→-/
    52→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    53→theorem erdos_1017 : answer(sorry) ↔
    54→    ∀ᶠ n in atTop,
    55→      ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (dG : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    56→        haveI := dG;
    57→        G.edgeFinset.card > n ^ 2 / 4 →
    58→          ∃ (k : ℕ) (parts : Fin k → Finset (Sym2 (Fin n))),
    59→            k < n ^ 2 / 4 ∧
    60→            (∀ i j : Fin k, i ≠ j → Disjoint (parts i) (parts j)) ∧
    61→            (∀ e, e ∈ G.edgeFinset ↔ ∃ i, e ∈ parts i) ∧
    62→            (∀ i : Fin k, ∃ (S : Finset (Fin n)),
    63→              G.IsClique (↑S : Set (Fin n)) ∧
    64→              parts i = S.offDiag.image (Quot.mk _)) := by
    65→  sorry
    66→
    67→/--
    68→Erdős–Goodman–Pósa theorem [EGP66]:
    69→
    70→Every simple graph on $n$ vertices can be decomposed into at most
    71→$\lfloor n^2/4 \rfloor$ edge-disjoint complete subgraphs.
    72→-/
    73→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    74→theorem erdos_1017.variants.erdos_goodman_posa :
    75→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (dG : DecidableRel G.Adj),
    76→      haveI := dG;
    77→      ∃ (k : ℕ) (parts : Fin k → Finset (Sym2 (Fin n))),
    78→        k ≤ n ^ 2 / 4 ∧
    79→        (∀ i j : Fin k, i ≠ j → Disjoint (parts i) (parts j)) ∧
    80→        (∀ e, e ∈ G.edgeFinset ↔ ∃ i, e ∈ parts i) ∧
    81→        (∀ i : Fin k, ∃ (S : Finset (Fin n)),
    82→          G.IsClique (↑S : Set (Fin n)) ∧
    83→          parts i = S.offDiag.image (Quot.mk _)) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos1017
    87→

    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 1040
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/1040](https://www.erdosproblems.com/1040)
    23→
    24→Let $F \subseteq \mathbb{C}$ be a closed infinite set, and let $\mu(F)$ be the infimum of
    25→$|\{z : |f(z)| < 1\}|$ (Lebesgue measure), as $f$ ranges over all polynomials of
    26→the shape $\prod(z - z_i)$ with $z_i \in F$.
    27→
    28→Is $\mu(F) = 0$ whenever the transfinite diameter of $F$ is $\geq 1$?
    29→
    30→The transfinite diameter (logarithmic capacity) of $F$ is defined by
    31→$$\rho(F) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sup_{z_1,\ldots,z_n \in F} \left(\prod_{i<j} |z_i - z_j|\right)^{1/\binom{n}{2}}.$$
    32→
    33→Erdős, Herzog, and Piranian showed the answer is yes when $F$ is a line segment
    34→or disc.
    35→
    36→[EHP58] Erdős, P., Herzog, F., and Piranian, G., *Metric properties of polynomials*,
    37→J. Analyse Math. **6** (1958), 125–148.
    38→-/
    39→
    40→open scoped ENNReal
    41→
    42→open MeasureTheory Classical Filter Finset
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos1040
    45→
    46→/-- The product of pairwise distances $\prod_{i<j} \|z_i - z_j\|$ for a tuple of
    47→complex numbers. -/
    48→noncomputable def pairwiseDistProd {n : ℕ} (z : Fin n → ℂ) : ℝ :=
    49→  ((univ : Finset (Fin n × Fin n)).filter (fun p => p.1 < p.2)).prod
    50→    (fun p => ‖z p.1 - z p.2‖)
    51→
    52→/-- The $n$-th transfinite diameter of $F \subseteq \mathbb{C}$:
    53→$d_n(F) = \sup_{z_1,\ldots,z_n \in F} \left(\prod_{i<j} |z_i - z_j|\right)^{2/(n(n-1))}$. -/
    54→noncomputable def nthTransfiniteDiam (F : Set ℂ) (n : ℕ) : ℝ :=
    55→  sSup {t : ℝ | ∃ z : Fin n → ℂ, (∀ i, z i ∈ F) ∧
    56→    t = (pairwiseDistProd z) ^ ((2 : ℝ) / (↑n * (↑n - 1)))}
    57→
    58→/-- The transfinite diameter (logarithmic capacity) of $F \subseteq \mathbb{C}$:
    59→$\rho(F) = \lim_{n \to \infty} d_n(F)$. -/
    60→noncomputable def transfiniteDiameter (F : Set ℂ) : ℝ :=
    61→  lim (atTop.map (fun n => nthTransfiniteDiam F n))
    62→
    63→/-- The sublevel set measure $\mu(F, \mu)$: infimum of $\mu(\{z : \|f(z)\| < 1\})$ over
    64→all monic polynomials with roots in $F$. Uses `Fin (n+1)` to ensure at least
    65→one root. -/
    66→noncomputable def sublevelMeasure (F : Set ℂ) (μ : Measure ℂ) : ℝ≥0∞ :=
    67→  ⨅ (n : ℕ) (z : Fin (n + 1) → ℂ) (_ : ∀ i, z i ∈ F),
    68→    μ {w : ℂ | ‖(univ : Finset (Fin (n + 1))).prod (fun i => w - z i)‖ < 1}
    69→
    70→/--
    71→Erdős Problem 1040 [EHP58, p.135]:
    72→
    73→Is it true that for every closed infinite set $F \subseteq \mathbb{C}$ with transfinite
    74→diameter $\geq 1$, the infimum of the Lebesgue measure of $\{z : |f(z)| < 1\}$
    75→over monic polynomials with all roots in $F$ is zero?
    76→-/
    77→@[category research open, AMS 28 30]
    78→theorem erdos_1040 : answer(sorry) ↔
    79→    ∀ (F : Set ℂ), IsClosed F → F.Infinite → transfiniteDiameter F ≥ 1 →
    80→    ∀ (μ : Measure ℂ) [μ.IsAddHaarMeasure], sublevelMeasure F μ = 0 := by
    81→  sorry
    82→
    83→end Erdos1040
    84→

    44→/-- `temp_v G v = deg(v)/(n(G) - deg(v))`. -/
    45→noncomputable def temp_v (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (v : α) : ℝ :=
    46→  let n := Fintype.card α
    47→  let deg := G.degree v

    49→
    50→/-- Maximum of `temp_v` over all vertices. -/
    51→noncomputable def MaxTemp (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] [Fintype α] [Nonempty α] : ℝ :=
    52→  let temps := Finset.univ.image (temp_v G)
    53→  temps.max' (Finset.image_nonempty.mpr Finset.univ_nonempty)
    54→
    55→/-- Cardinality of the union of the neighbourhoods of the ends of the non-edge `e`. -/
    56→def non_edge_neighborhood_card (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] (e : Sym2 α) : ℕ :=

    61→noncomputable def NG (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=

   104→/-- Distance from a vertex to a finite set. -/
   105→noncomputable def distToSet (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) (S : Set α) : ℕ :=
   106→  if h : S.toFinset.Nonempty then
   107→    (S.toFinset.image (fun s => G.dist v s)).min' (Finset.Nonempty.image h _)
   108→  else 0
   109→
   110→/-- Average distance of `G`. -/
   111→noncomputable def averageDistance (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
   112→  if Fintype.card α > 1 then
   113→    (∑ u ∈ Finset.univ, ∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, (G.dist u v : ℝ)) /
   114→      ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) * ((Fintype.card α : ℝ) - 1))
   115→  else
   116→    0
   117→
   118→/-- The floor of the average distance of `G`. -/
   119→noncomputable def path (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=

   140→def IsPathCover (G : SimpleGraph α) (P : Finset (Finset α)) : Prop :=

   158→noncomputable def szeged_aux (G : SimpleGraph α) (u v : α) : ℕ :=
   159→  (Finset.univ.filter (fun w => G.edist w u < G.edist w v)).card
   160→
   161→/-- The Szeged index of `G`.

   164→`n_u(uv)` is the number of vertices closer to `u` than `v`.

   166→noncomputable def szegedIndex (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   167→  ∑ e ∈ G.edgeFinset,

   171→noncomputable def averageDegree (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℚ  :=

   177→
   178→/-- The annihilation number of a graph. This is the largest number of degrees that can be added
   179→together without going over the total number of edges of that graph. -/
   180→def annihilationNumber (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   181→  -- Calculate the limit: The number of edges (Sum of degrees / 2)
   182→  letI limit := G.edgeFinset.card
   183→
   184→  -- The set of all multisets of degrees that sum to less than or equal to `limit`
   185→  Finset.Iic (degreeMultiset G)
   186→    |>.filter (fun S ↦ Multiset.sum S ≤ limit)
   187→    |>.sup Multiset.card
   188→
   189→/--
   190→Computes the annihilation number of a graph G.
   191→
   192→It calculates the degree sequence, sorts it ascendingly, and finds the largest prefix length 'k'
   193→(where `0 ≤ k ≤ |V(G)|`) such that the sum of the prefix is less than or equal to the sum of the corresponding suffix.
   194→-/
   195→noncomputable def annihilationNumber' (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   196→  -- 1. Get the degree sequence sorted in ascending order.
   197→  -- G.degree_list returns the list of degrees.
   198→  letI degrees := (Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
   199→
   200→  -- 2. Define the condition for the annihilation number.
   201→  -- k represents the number of smallest degrees considered (the length of the prefix).
   202→  letI condition (k : ℕ) : Bool := (degrees.take k).sum ≤ (degrees.drop k).sum
   203→
   204→  -- 3. Find the maximum k in {0, ..., n} satisfying the condition.
   205→  -- List.range (n + 1) generates the list [0, 1, ..., n].
   206→  letI candidates := (List.range (Fintype.card α + 1)).filter condition
   207→
   208→  -- 4. Return the maximum candidate.
   209→  -- The list of candidates is guaranteed to be non-empty because k=0 always satisfies
   210→  -- the condition (0 ≤ sum of all degrees).
   211→  candidates.getLast!
   212→
   213→set_option linter.unusedSectionVars false in
   214→-- TODO(Paul-Lez): debug the issue with the unused variable linter...
   215→proof_wanted annihilationNumberEq (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] :
   216→    annihilationNumber G = annihilationNumber' G
   217→
   218→/-!
   219→## Residue
   220→The residue of a graph is the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   221→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   222→-/
   223→
   224→/--
   225→Helper function: Performs one step of the Havel-Hakimi reduction on a degree sequence.
   226→Assumes the input list `s` is sorted descending.
   227→Removes the first element `d`, decrements the next `d` elements by 1, and re-sorts the list descending.
   228→
   229→Note: when `s` is the list of vertices arising from a simple graph, if the first index is `s` then
   230→the degree list always has length at least `s+1` so this makes sense.
   231→-/
   232→private def havelHakimiStep (s : List ℕ) : List ℕ :=
   233→  match s with
   234→  | [] => []
   235→  | d :: rest =>
   236→    -- Split the rest into the part to decrement (first d elements) and the remaining part.
   237→    let (to_decrement, remaining) := rest.splitAt d
   238→    -- Decrement the elements
   239→    let decremented := to_decrement.map (· - 1)
   240→    -- Combine and re-sort descending.
   241→    (decremented ++ remaining).mergeSort (· ≥ ·)
   242→
   243→/--
   244→Auxiliary function to calculate the residue recursively.
   245→Applies Havel-Hakimi steps until the sequence consists only of zeros or is empty.
   246→-/
   247→private partial def residueAux : List ℕ → ℕ
   248→  | [] => 0        -- Empty sequence, residue is 0.
   249→  | 0 :: s => 1 + s.length -- If the largest degree is 0 (and the list is sorted), all are 0.
   250→  | s => residueAux (havelHakimiStep s) -- Apply one reduction step and recurse.
   251→
   252→/--
   253→Computes the residue of a graph G, ,i.e. the number of zeros remaining after iteratively applying the Havel-Hakimi
   254→algorithm to the degree sequence until all remaining degrees are zero.
   255→Starts with the descending degree sequence and applies the Havel-Hakimi process.
   256→-/
   257→noncomputable def residue (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   258→  -- Get the degree sequence sorted in descending order and apply `residueAux`.
   259→  residueAux ((Finset.univ.image fun v => G.degree v).sort (· ≥ ·))
   260→
   261→/--
   262→Fractional alpha. This is defined as
   263→$$
   264→\max_x \sum_{v \in V} x_v
   265→$$
   266→subject to $0 \le x_v \le 1$ for all $v \in V$ and
   267→$x_u + x_v \le 1$ whenever $(u, v)$ are connected by an edge.
   268→-/
   269→noncomputable def fractionalAlpha (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   270→  sSup {(∑ i, x i) | (x : α → ℝ) (_ : ∀ v, x v ∈ Set.Icc 0 1)
   271→    (_ : ∀ u v, G.Adj u v → x u + x v ≤ 1)}
   272→
   273→/--
   274→Lovász Theta Function (ϑ(G))
   275→The Lovász theta function is defined as:
   276→ϑ(G) = min λ_max(A)
   277→where the minimum is taken over all symmetric matrices A such that:
   278→
   279→A_ij = 1 for all i = j (diagonal entries are 1)
   280→A_ij = 0 for all {i,j} ∈ E (entries corresponding to edges are 0)
   281→A is positive semidefinite
   282→
   283→Here λ_max(A) denotes the maximum eigenvalue of A.
   284→-/
   285→noncomputable def lovaczThetaFunction
   286→    (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
   287→  sInf {(Matrix.IsHermitian.maxEigenvalue hA) | (A : Matrix α α ℝ) (hA : A.IsHermitian)
   288→      (_ : ∀ i, A i i = 1) (_ : ∀ i j, G.Adj i j → A i j = 0)}
   289→
   290→/--
   291→Given a simple graph `G` with adjacency matrix `A`, this is the number `n₀ + min n₊ n₋` where:
   292→- `n₀` is the multiplicity of zero as an eigenvalue of `A`
   293→- `n₊` is the number of positive eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   294→- `n₋` is the number of negative eigenvalues of `A` (counting multiplicities)
   295→-/
   296→noncomputable def cvetkovic (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
   297→  letI A : Matrix α α ℝ := G.adjMatrix ℝ
   298→  letI spectrum : Multiset ℝ := (Matrix.charpoly A).roots
   299→  letI positive_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 < x)
   300→  letI negative_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 > x)
   301→  letI zero_count : ℕ := spectrum.countP (fun x => 0 = x)
   302→  zero_count + min positive_count negative_count
   303→
   304→end SimpleGraph
   305→

    16→
    17→import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Combinatorics.AP.Basic
    18→import Mathlib.Analysis.Normed.Field.Lemmas
    19→import Mathlib.Order.CompletePartialOrder
    20→
    21→open Function Set
    22→open scoped Pointwise
    23→
    24→variable {α : Type*} [AddCommMonoid α]
    25→
    26→/--
    27→A set $A$ is said to be sum-free if the sumset $A + A$ is disjoint from $A$, i.e.
    28→if the equation $a + b = c$ has no solution with $a, b, c \in A$.
    29→-/
    30→def IsSumFree (A : Set α) : Prop := Disjoint (A + A) A
    31→
    32→/-- A set $A$ of natural numbers is said to have bounded gaps if there exists an integer $p$ such
    33→that $A ∩ [n, n + 1, ..., n + p]$ is nonempty for all $n$. -/
    34→def IsSyndetic (A : Set ℕ) : Prop := ∃ p, ∀ n, (A ∩ .Icc n (n + p)).Nonempty
    35→
    36→/-- A Sidon set is a set, such that such that all pairwise sums of elements are distinct apart from
    37→coincidences forced by the commutativity of addition. -/
    38→def IsSidon (A : Set α) : Prop := ∀ᵉ (i₁ ∈ A) (j₁ ∈ A) (i₂ ∈ A) (j₂ ∈ A),
    39→  i₁ + i₂ = j₁ + j₂ → (i₁ = j₁ ∧ i₂ = j₂) ∨ (i₁ = j₂ ∧ i₂ = j₁)
    40→
    41→namespace Set
    42→
    43→lemma IsSidon.avoids_isAPOfLength_three {A : Set ℕ} (hA : IsSidon A)
    44→    {Y : Set ℕ} (hY : Y.IsAPOfLength 3) :
    45→    (A ∩ Y).ncard ≤ 2 := by
    46→  simp [IsAPOfLength, IsAPOfLengthWith] at hY
    47→  obtain ⟨hc, ⟨a, d, hY⟩⟩ := hY
    48→  have hY_card : Y.ncard = 3 := by simp [ncard, hc]
    49→  by_contra! h
    50→  have hss : Y ⊆ A ∩ Y := by
    51→    have hY_fin : Finite Y := finite_of_ncard_ne_zero (by linarith)
    52→    rw [Set.eq_of_subset_of_ncard_le (Set.inter_subset_right) (by linarith)]
    53→  have ha : a ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨0, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    54→  have ha₁ : a + d ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨1, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    55→  have ha₂ : a + 2 • d ∈ A := mem_of_mem_inter_left <| hss (hY ▸ ⟨2, by norm_num, by simp⟩)
    56→  have := hA _ ha _ ha₁ _ ha₂ _ ha₁ (by simp; omega)
    57→  simp at this
    58→  simp [hY, this.1, setOf_and] at hY_card
    59→  linarith [ncard_singleton _ ▸ ncard_inter_le_ncard_right {a | ∃ x, x < 3} {a}]
    60→
    61→theorem IsSidon.subset {A B : Set α} (hB : IsSidon B) (hAB : A ⊆ B) : IsSidon A :=
    62→  fun _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ ↦ hB _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) _ (hAB ‹_›) ‹_›
    63→
    64→theorem IsSidon.insert {A : Set α} {m : α} [IsRightCancelAdd α] [IsLeftCancelAdd α]
    65→    (hA : IsSidon A) :
    66→    IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) ↔ (m ∈ A ∨ ∀ᵉ (a ∈ A) (b ∈ A), m + m ≠ a + b ∧ ∀ c ∈ A, m + a ≠ b + c) := by
    67→  by_cases h_mem : m ∈ A
    68→  · exact ⟨fun _ ↦ .inl h_mem, fun _ ↦ by rwa [union_singleton, insert_eq_of_mem h_mem]⟩
    69→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ .inr fun a ha b hb ↦ ⟨fun hc ↦ ?_, fun c hc h_contr ↦ ?_⟩, fun hm ↦ ?_⟩
    70→  · exact h m (by simp) a (by simp [ha]) m (by simp) b (by simp [hb]) hc
    71→      |>.elim (fun _ ↦ by simp_all) (fun _ ↦ by simp_all)
    72→  · exact h m (by simp) b (by simp [hb]) a (by simp [ha]) c (by simp [hc]) h_contr
    73→      |>.elim (fun _ ↦ by simp_all) (fun _ ↦ by simp_all)
    74→  · intro i₁ hi₁
    75→    rcases hi₁ with (hi₁ | hi₁)
    76→    · intro j₁ hj₁
    77→      rcases hj₁ with (hj₁ | hj₁)
    78→      · intro i₂ hi₂
    79→        rcases hi₂ with (hi₂ | hi₂)
    80→        · intro j₂ hj₂
    81→          rcases hj₂ with (hj₂ | hj₂)
    82→          · exact fun h ↦ hA i₁ hi₁ j₁ hj₁ i₂ hi₂ j₂ hj₂ h
    83→          · simp_all
    84→            exact fun h ↦ by cases (hm j₁ hj₁ i₁ hi₁).2 i₂ hi₂ (add_comm j₁ m ▸ h.symm)
    85→        · simp_all
    86→          exact fun a ha h ↦ by cases (hm i₁ hi₁ j₁ hj₁).2 a ha (add_comm i₁ m ▸ h)
    87→      · simp_all
    88→        refine ⟨fun b hb h ↦ .inr <| by simp_all [add_comm], fun b hb ↦ ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, ?_⟩⟩
    89→        · cases (hm i₁ hi₁ b hb).1 h.symm
    90→        · exact fun c hc h ↦ by cases ((hm c hc i₁ hi₁).2 b hb) h.symm
    91→    · simp_all
    92→      exact fun _ _ _ _ _ ↦ by simp_all [add_comm]
    93→
    94→
    95→/-!
    96→Maximal Sidon sets in an interval.
    97→
    98→We follow the convention that `IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N` means `A ⊆ {1, …, N}` is Sidon and
    99→is inclusion-maximal among subsets of `Set.Icc 1 N` with the Sidon property.
   100→-/
   101→
   102→/-- `IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N` means `A ⊆ {1, …, N}` is Sidon and cannot be extended within
   103→`{1, …, N}` while remaining Sidon. -/
   104→def IsMaximalSidonSetIn (A : Set ℕ) (N : ℕ) : Prop :=
   105→  A ⊆ Set.Icc 1 N ∧ IsSidon A ∧
   106→    ∀ ⦃x : ℕ⦄, x ∈ Set.Icc 1 N → x ∉ A → ¬ IsSidon (A ∪ {x})
   107→
   108→namespace IsMaximalSidonSetIn
   109→
   110→/-- If `A` is a maximal Sidon set in `{1, …, N}`, then `A ⊆ {1, …, N}`. -/
   111→theorem subset {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) :
   112→    A ⊆ Set.Icc 1 N := hA.1
   113→
   114→/-- If `A` is a maximal Sidon set in `{1, …, N}`, then `A` is Sidon. -/
   115→theorem isSidon {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) : IsSidon A := hA.2.1
   116→
   117→/-- Maximality condition unpacked. -/
   118→theorem maximal {A : Set ℕ} {N : ℕ} (hA : IsMaximalSidonSetIn A N) {x : ℕ}
   119→    (hx : x ∈ Set.Icc 1 N) (hxA : x ∉ A) : ¬ IsSidon (A ∪ {x}) := hA.2.2 hx hxA
   120→
   121→end IsMaximalSidonSetIn
   122→
   123→end Set
   124→
   125→namespace Finset
   126→
   127→instance (A : Finset α) [DecidableEq α] : Decidable (IsSidon (A : Set α)) :=
   128→  decidable_of_iff (∀ᵉ (i₁ ∈ A) (j₁ ∈ A) (i₂ ∈ A) (j₂ ∈ A), _) <| by rfl
   129→
   130→/-- The maximum size of a Sidon set in the supplied `Finset`. -/
   131→def maxSidonSubsetCard (A : Finset α) [DecidableEq α] : ℕ :=
   132→  (A.powerset.filter fun B : Finset α ↦ IsSidon (B : Set α)).sup Finset.card
   133→
   134→/-- If `A` is finite Sidon, then `A ∪ {s}` is also Sidon provided `s ≥ A.max + 1`. -/
   135→theorem IsSidon.insert_ge_max' {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) {s : ℕ}
   136→    (hs : 2 * A.max' h + 1 ≤ s) :
   137→    IsSidon (A ∪ {s}) := by
   138→  have h₁ {a b c : ℕ} (ha : a ∈ A) (hb : b ∈ A) (hc : c ∈ A) :
   139→        a + b < 2 * A.max' h + 1 + c := by linarith [A.le_max' _ ha, A.le_max' _ hb]
   140→  have : s ∉ A := by
   141→    exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   142→      linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   143→  exact (IsSidon.insert hA).2 <| by simpa [this] using fun a ha b hb ↦
   144→    ⟨by linarith [A.le_max' _ ha, A.le_max' _ hb], fun c hc ↦ by linarith [h₁ hc hb ha]⟩
   145→
   146→theorem IsSidon.exists_insert {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) :
   147→    ∃ m ∉ A, IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) := by
   148→  refine ⟨2 * A.max' h + 1, ?_, insert_ge_max' h hA le_rfl⟩
   149→  exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   150→    linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   151→
   152→theorem IsSidon.exists_insert_ge {A : Finset ℕ} (h : A.Nonempty) (hA : IsSidon (A : Set ℕ)) (s : ℕ) :
   153→    ∃ m ≥ s, m ∉ A ∧ IsSidon (A ∪ {m}) := by
   154→  refine ⟨if s ≥ 2 * A.max' h + 1 then s else 2 * A.max' h + 1, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
   155→  · split_ifs <;> linarith
   156→  · split_ifs <;>
   157→    exact mt (A.le_max' _) <| not_le.2 <| Finset.max'_lt_iff _ ‹_› |>.2 fun a ha ↦ by
   158→      linarith [A.le_max' _ ha]
   159→  · split_ifs with hs
   160→    · exact insert_ge_max' h hA hs
   161→    · exact insert_ge_max' h hA le_rfl
   162→
   163→end Finset
   164→

    14→limitations under the License.

    16→
    17→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Acyclic
    18→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Bipartite
    19→import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Matching
    20→import Mathlib.Data.Real.Archimedean
    21→import Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.PiL2
    22→
    23→namespace SimpleGraph
    24→
    25→open Classical Finset List
    26→
    27→variable {α : Type*} [Fintype α] [DecidableEq α]
    28→
    29→/-- `Ls G` is the maximum number of leaves over all spanning trees of `G`.
    30→It is defined to be `0` when `G` is not connected. -/
    31→noncomputable def Ls (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    32→  letI spanningTrees := { T : Subgraph G | T.IsSpanning ∧ IsTree T.coe }
    33→  letI leaves (T : Subgraph G) := T.verts.toFinset.filter (fun v => T.degree v = 1)
    34→  letI num_leaves (T : Subgraph G) := (leaves T).card
    35→  sSup (Set.image (fun T => (num_leaves T : ℝ)) spanningTrees)
    36→
    37→/-- `n G` is the number of vertices of `G` as a real number. -/
    38→noncomputable def n (_ : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ := Fintype.card α
    39→
    40→/-- `m G` is the size of a maximum matching of `G`. -/
    41→noncomputable def m (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    42→  let matchings := { M : Subgraph G | M.IsMatching }
    43→  sSup (Set.image (fun M => (M.edgeSet.toFinset.card : ℝ)) matchings)
    44→
    45→/-- The maximum cardinality among all independent sets `s`
    46→    that maximize the quantity `|s| - |N(s)|`, where `N(s)`
    47→    is the neighborhood of the set `s`. -/
    48→noncomputable def aprime (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℝ :=
    49→  letI indep_sets : Finset (Finset α) := univ.powerset.filter (fun s => G.IsIndepSet (s : Set α))
    50→  letI diff (s : Finset α) : ℤ := (s.card : ℤ) - (⋃ v ∈ (s : Set α), G.neighborSet v).toFinset.card
    51→  letI max_diff := (indep_sets.image diff).max
    52→  letI critical_sets := indep_sets.filter (fun s ↦ diff s = max_diff.getD 0)
    53→  letI max_card := (critical_sets.image Finset.card).max
    54→  (max_card.getD 0 : ℝ)
    55→
    56→/-- `largestInducedForestSize G` is the size of a largest induced forest of `G`. -/
    57→noncomputable def largestInducedForestSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    58→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsAcyclic ∧ s.card = n }
    59→
    60→/-- The degree sequence of a graph, sorted in nondecreasing order. -/
    61→noncomputable def degreeSequence (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : List ℕ :=
    62→  (Finset.univ.val.map fun v : α => G.degree v).sort (· ≤ ·)
    63→
    64→/--
    65→The maximum number of occurrences of any term of the degree sequence of `G`.
    66→-/
    67→noncomputable def degreeSequenceMultiplicity (G : SimpleGraph α) [DecidableRel G.Adj] : ℕ :=
    68→  letI degs := degreeSequence G
    69→  (List.max? (degs.map (fun d => degs.count d))).getD 0
    70→
    71→/-- `largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G` is the size of a largest induced
    72→bipartite subgraph of `G`. -/
    73→noncomputable def largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℕ :=
    74→  sSup { n | ∃ s : Finset α, (G.induce s).IsBipartite ∧ s.card = n }
    75→
    76→/-- `b G` is the number of vertices of a largest induced bipartite subgraph of `G`.
    77→Returned as a real number. -/
    78→noncomputable def b (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    79→  (largestInducedBipartiteSubgraphSize G : ℝ)
    80→
    81→/-- Independence number of the neighbourhood of `v`. -/
    82→noncomputable def indepNeighborsCard (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℕ :=
    83→  (G.induce (G.neighborSet v)).indepNum
    84→
    85→/-- The same quantity as a real number. -/
    86→noncomputable def indepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) (v : α) : ℝ :=
    87→  (indepNeighborsCard G v : ℝ)
    88→
    89→/-- Average of `indepNeighbors` over all vertices. -/
    90→noncomputable def averageIndepNeighbors (G : SimpleGraph α) : ℝ :=
    91→  (∑ v ∈ Finset.univ, indepNeighbors G v) / (Fintype.card α : ℝ)
    92→
    93→/-- A unit distance graph in ℝ²:
    94→A graph where the vertices V are a collection of points in ℝ² and there is
    95→an edge between two points if and only if the distance between them is 1. -/
    96→def UnitDistancePlaneGraph (V : Set (EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2))) : SimpleGraph V where
    97→  Adj x y := Dist.dist x y = 1
    98→  symm x y := by simp [PseudoMetricSpace.dist_comm]
    99→
   100→/--
   101→Two walks are internally disjoint if they share no vertices other than their endpoints.
   102→-/
   103→def InternallyDisjoint {V : Type*} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v x y : V}
   104→    (p : G.Walk u v) (q : G.Walk x y) : Prop :=
   105→  Disjoint p.support.tail.dropLast q.support.tail.dropLast
   106→
   107→/--
   108→We say a graph is infinitely connected if any two vertices are connected by infinitely many
   109→pairwise disjoint paths.
   110→-/
   111→def InfinitelyConnected {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : Prop :=
   112→  Pairwise fun u v ↦ ∃ P : Set (G.Walk u v),
   113→    P.Infinite ∧ (∀ p ∈ P, p.IsPath) ∧ P.Pairwise InternallyDisjoint
   114→
   115→/-- Infinite graphs: definitions for max degree and clique number so that the maximum
   116→degree (resp. clique number) of a graph with unbounded degree (resp. clique size) is
   117→`∞` rather than 0.
   118→-/
   119→noncomputable
   120→def edegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) (v : V) : ℕ∞ := (G.neighborSet v).encard
   121→
   122→noncomputable
   123→def emaxDegree {V : Type*} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ v, G.edegree v
   124→
   125→noncomputable
   126→def ecliqueNum {V : Type} (G : SimpleGraph V) : ℕ∞ := ⨆ (s : Finset V) (_ : G.IsClique s), #s
   127→
   128→end SimpleGraph
   129→
